description:
You are helping a developer keep track of the source-code files in a small project.
    To do so, the developer wants a tiny SQLite database that records each file’s
    name and absolute path.

    1. Work inside the directory /home/user/projects  
       (create it if it does not already exist).

    2. Using only the **sqlite3** command-line client, create a new database file
       called /home/user/projects/file_index.db .

    3. Still in sqlite3, run the following operations **in this order**:  
       a. Create a table named project_files with exactly these columns and types  
          • id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY  
          • filename TEXT NOT NULL  
          • path TEXT NOT NULL  
       b. Insert exactly three rows, one per file:
          1) id = 1, filename = "main.py",      path = "/home/user/projects/src/main.py"  
          2) id = 2, filename = "utils.py",     path = "/home/user/projects/src/utils.py"  
          3) id = 3, filename = "README.md",    path = "/home/user/projects/README.md"

    4. After inserting the data, run a single SQL query that selects all columns
       from project_files, ordered by id ascending, and redirect the exact
       console output of that SELECT into the text file  
       /home/user/projects/file_index.log .

       Required output format inside file_index.log (including spacing):
         id|filename|path
         1|main.py|/home/user/projects/src/main.py
         2|utils.py|/home/user/projects/src/utils.py
         3|README.md|/home/user/projects/README.md

       Notes about the format:
       • The very first line must be the header produced by `sqlite3` when the
         `.mode` is left at its default of “list”.  
       • Column separators must be literal pipe characters (|) with no extra
         spaces.  
       • Each record must be on its own newline.  
       • There must be exactly four lines in total (one header + three data rows)
         and a trailing newline after the last line.

    5. When everything is finished, you should have two new artefacts:
       • /home/user/projects/file_index.db  – a valid SQLite database containing
         the table and data described above.  
       • /home/user/projects/file_index.log – the query output that exactly
         matches the required four-line, pipe-separated format.

    Do not place any other files in /home/user/projects.  The automated grader
    will open the database to verify its schema and rows, and it will compare the
    contents of file_index.log byte-for-byte against the format shown above.
truth:
Expected artefacts after the agent completes the task:

    1. /home/user/projects/file_index.db  
       • Contains one table named project_files.  
       • Schema SQL:
         CREATE TABLE project_files(
             id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
             filename TEXT NOT NULL,
             path TEXT NOT NULL
         );
       • Exactly three rows:
         (1, "main.py",  "/home/user/projects/src/main.py")
         (2, "utils.py", "/home/user/projects/src/utils.py")
         (3, "README.md","/home/user/projects/README.md")

    2. /home/user/projects/file_index.log  
       Exact ASCII contents (including the final newline):
id|filename|path
1|main.py|/home/user/projects/src/main.py
2|utils.py|/home/user/projects/src/utils.py
3|README.md|/home/user/projects/README.md

    3. Directory /home/user/projects is user-writable (mode 755 or 775 is fine).
    4. No additional files, tables, or rows may exist.
